title: France records its hottest day with relief on the horizon
subhed: With an average day-night temperature of 30°C, June 24 set a new heat record in France. Forecasters expect a gradual end to the heatwave in the western part of the country by the end of the week. High temperatures will persist until next Tuesday or Wednesday in the rest of the country.

Ukrainian strikes on Russian refineries are triggering shortages and rising discontent
subhed: For the past three months, Kyiv's drones have targeted Russian refineries, ports, depots and pipelines, causing ripple effects throughout the Russian economy. Gasoline has become scarce at service stations.

title: Two killed in Israeli strike in South Lebanon, Hezbollah denounces true 'violation'
subhed: Two people were killed in southern Lebanon on Wednesday in an Israeli drone strike that Hezbollah said was the latest breach of a ceasefire agreement between the two sides.

title: China remains on the sidelines of the World Cup
subhed: The global superpower, which relishes the opportunity to excel at major international events, has only managed to qualify once, in 2002. Despite Xi Jinping's determination, the poor performance of the national football team continues to disappoint Chinese fans.

title: 250 years of American independence: A groundbreaking Constitution for 'a more perfect union'
subhed: 'America 250' (3/13). Eleven years after the founding of the United States, there was still much left to invent. The Constitution, adopted in September 1787, offered an unprecedented balance between the three branches of government in order to achieve 'a more perfect union' of the people. The first president, George Washington, elected in 1789, remains revered to this day.

Living under Paris's zinc roofs in 40°C heat: 'One day, the people living there will look for those responsible'
subhed: As France faces extreme heat, many of these top-story spaces have become uninhabitable. Some residents have formed groups to call on their landlords, neighbors and local authorities to address their living conditions.

title: France's public pools need urgent renovation: 'What would we do without them? We wouldn't make it'
subhed: Aging, energy-intensive and expensive for rural towns to maintain, many aquatic centers in the countryside have been shutting down. Yet these facilities provide equal access for people to cool off and learn to swim. An MP recently sounded the alarm about the worrying situation facing these essential local public services.

title: The (not so) stupid questions to ask during a heatwave in France
subhed: Can we swim in the Seine? Should I eat meat when it is 40 degrees? Can I ask for a glass of water in any café? Le Monde answers your questions.
